Introduction
Gastrointestinal (GI) dysfunction is most often experienced as a 
conglomeration of symptoms that indicate lower GI impairment, 
including constipation, diarrhea, and fecal incontinence (FI).  
It can also present as upper GI impairment heralded by bloating, 
nausea, early satiety, burning, and gaseousness. Despite its severity, 
neurogenic bowel dysfunction (NBD) can be a clinically elusive 
condition. It is often eclipsed by other, more noticeable associated 
motor deficits, but can be life-limiting if not thoroughly assessed 
and treated using rehabilitation principles. Interdisciplinary reha-
bilitative interventions for NBD focus on establishing a total 
management plan for bowel function, termed a bowel program, and 
for assisted defecation, known as bowel care.
129 Because sensation 
and mobility are often restricted, limiting the person’s ability to 
anticipate the need for and to physically perform independent 
bowel care and hygiene, the overarching rehabilitation approach to 
this complex, multisystem intersection of impairments must be 
holistic and have as a goal the design of a bowel program that is 
liberating and individualized for the patient.
It is surprising that of the many abilities regained during the 
rehabilitation process, bowel care capabilities at the time of dis -
charge are not always comparable with other skills that would be 
expected for a given level of function. Bowel continence is one of 
the greatest predictors of return to home for stroke survivors.
31 
More than one-third of surveyed persons with spinal cord injury 
(SCI) rated bowel and bladder dysfunction as having the most 
significant impact on their lives after SCI, and yet bowel manage-
ment has been found to be one of the areas of least competence 
among rehabilitated spinal cord–injured persons.
52 In a Swedish 
review of medical problems after SCI, 41% of subjects rated 
bowel dysfunction as a moderately to severely life-limiting  
components of holistic care. Recognition of all aspects of 
impairment and disability that limit a person’s ability to 
volitionally defecate and maintain continence must be 
assessed and addressed to optimize GI function. Prevention 
and early intervention of more serious medical problems 
associated with increased clinic and hospital visits are 
imperative to maintain health, good quality of life, and general 
well-being.
• Management of NBD symptoms and problems (constipation, 
fecal incontinence, abdominal and pelvic pain) must be 
established on strong partnerships between patients, their 
caregivers, and medical providers to provide personalized care 
based on real-world factors, self-management tools, and 
adherence to joint goals of care. This must be reviewed and 
updated periodically to account for neurodegenerative and 
age-related effects.
22
Neurogenic Bowel:  
Dysfunction and Rehabilitation
GIANNA M. RODRIGUEZ, LANCE GOETZ, AND STEVEN A. STIENS †
• GI dysfunction is prevalent with neurologic diseases affecting 
the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nervous system.
• Normal functioning of the GI tract requires coordination of 
muscle contraction, digestion and absorption of nutrients, 
preparation and movement of waste, voluntary defecation, and 
adequate elimination of stool; all regulated by interactions with 
the brain and spinal cord, autonomic nervous system and 
enteric nervous system (ENS), and somatomotor system.
• Management of NBD involves a stepwise method that 
commences with conservative measures including minimizing 
constipating medications, good intake of fluids and fiber, to 
utilization of oral laxatives, rectal medications, strategies for 
bowel evacuation, and can progress to more invasive measures 
and surgical procedures.
• Interdisciplinary rehabilitative interventions for NBD from the 
onset of neurologic compromise and throughout life are key 
SUMMARY PEARLS

problem.84 It is obvious that bowel management is an important 
area that is often undermanaged.
Epidemiology
GI dysfunction is prevalent in patients with neurologic diseases, 
particularly those who require rehabilitation. In addition to the 
direct effects of neurologic disease on gut function; debility, insuf-
ficient fluid intake, and use of anticholinergics and other medi -
cations can play complicating roles in exacerbation of enteric 
problems. The common digestive tract problems and NBD found 
in individuals with stroke, brain injury, multiple sclerosis, Parkinson 
disease, neuromuscular diseases, muscular dystrophy, dysauto -
nomias, peripheral nerve injuries, SCI, myelomeningocele, and 
other neurologic disorders have been shown to be difficult, chal -
lenging to manage, and often the primary disabling and handi -
capping feature for patients.
a,90 Solving bowel problems signifi-
cantly enhances quality of life.a,90
NBD results from autonomic and somatic denervation and 
commonly produces FI, constipation, and difficulty with evacua-
tion (DWE). The prevalence of FI and fecal impaction ranges 
from 0.3% to 5.0% in the general population. The prevalence  
of DWE ranges from 10% to 50% among the hospitalized or 
institutionalized elderly.
135 Although many GI disorders can con-
tribute to FI or DWE, disorders that impair the extrinsic (sympa-
thetic, parasympathetic, or somatic) nervous control of the bowel 
and anorectal mechanisms are more common among the patient 
populations seen by physiatrists.
Impact
Living with nausea, vomiting, bloating, abdominal pain, consti-
pation, diarrhea, or FI profoundly impacts quality of life. It sig -
nificantly affects nutrition, overall health, and sense of well-being. 
Loss of voluntary control over bowel function alters every facet of 
a person’s life at home, at work, and in the community. The abil-
ity to spontaneously regulate and direct bowel function is essential 
for participation in everyday activities at home, work, or school. 
It plays a major role in one’s ability to create and maintain rela-
tionships. It has a significant impact on such activities as recre -
ation and travel. Anticipation, experience and memory of epi -
sodes of extremes of NBD can create considerable psychological, 
social, and emotional trauma.
Incontinence, including of stool, has been repeatedly identified 
as a major factor for institutionalization. FI decreases return-to-
home rates for stroke patients. Nursing home costs are higher for 
patients with FI.
135 Almost one-third of persons with SCI report or 
exhibit worsening of bowel function 5 years beyond their injury 
with 33% developing megacolon, suggesting inadequate long-term 
management.
53,132 Of note, evidence has shown some improvement 
in outcomes for SCI bowel management.70 When restoring normal 
defecation is not possible, social continence becomes the goal. Social 
continence is defined as predictable, scheduled, adequate defeca -
tions without incontinence at other times. It is often achievable by 
persons with NBD. Embarrassment and humiliation from FI fre-
quently result in vocational and social disability, which adds sub-
stantial costs to the care related to NBD.
Neuroanatomy and Physiology of the 
Gastrointestinal Tract
In the past two decades, there has been significant advancement 
in the science of neurogastroenterology. This includes new discov-
eries in the basic physiology of the GI tract and interactions with 
the brain and spinal cord, autonomic nervous system and enteric 
nervous system (ENS), and somatomotor system (pharyngeal 
muscles and swallowing, pelvic floor muscles and defecation, 
continence and pelvic pain). Normal functioning of the stomach 
and intestines requires coordination of muscle contraction, diges-
tion and absorption of nutrients, and regulation of blood flow. 
The neural schema of the GI tract is much more complex than 
initially thought. Neural control of the GI tract is an extremely 
organized and integrated hierarchy of mechanisms that involve 
the central nervous system (CNS; brain, spinal cord), autonomic 
nervous system (sympathetic and parasympathetic), and the ENS 
(Figs. 22.1 and 22.2).
151,152
Enteric Nervous System
The ENS is a distinct network that has its own set of neurons 
that coordinate sensory and motor functions. In the ENS, the

ganglia are interconnected, which allows for integration and 
processing of data (as opposed to autonomic ganglia, which only 
serve as relay centers for stimuli transmitted from the CNS). 
There are three different types of neurons in the ENS, which  
are sensory neurons, interneurons, and motor neurons.
151,152 
Sensory neurons are triggered by thermal, chemical, or mechani -
cal stimuli and transform these sensations into action potentials 
that are conducted into the gut wall network. Interneurons serve 
as conduits between the sensory and motor neurons. The numer-
ous synapses between interneurons create a highly organized 
circuitry that processes sensory input from the gut and other 
parts of the nervous system and integrates and generates reflex 
responses to these stimuli. Motor neurons are the final common 
pathway. They receive and translate signals to the gut (mucosa, 
muscle, vasculature) that affect digestive, interdigestive (i.e.,  
between meals), and emetic functions based on the transmitters 
released.

It is now known that there is automatic feedback control  
in the ENS, with the neurons being in close proximity to the 
stomach and intestines. This can be manifested as reflex circuits 
that systematize reflex responses to sensory signals, integrative 
circuits that coordinate motor patterns (migrating motor com-
plex [MMC], digestive activity, giant migratory contractions 
[GMCs]),
151,152 or as a pattern generating activity that is gener -
ated when a “command neuron” is actuated and a rhythmic, 
repetitive behavior occurs.
152 The ENS is the key to proper 
functioning of the entire GI tract. This collection of highly  
organized neurons is situated in two primary layers: the submu -
cosal (Meissner) plexus and the intramuscular myenteric  
(Auerbach) plexus. Combined, these plexi interconnect an esti -
mated 10 to 100 million neurons, plus two to three glial cells 
per neuron. The ENS glial cells resemble CNS astrocytes and are 
much less abundant than the 20 to 50 glial cells per neuron in 
the CNS.
45 The coordination of segment-to-segment function

is largely regulated by the ENS. 148 The ENS also has its own 
blood-nerve barrier, similar to the blood-brain barrier of  
the CNS.108
Enteric Nervous System: Relationships to  
the Spinal Cord and Brain
Sensory information from vagal afferents in the ENS is relayed to 
the nodose ganglia (caudal ganglion of the vagus) and conse -
quently to the nucleus tractus solitarius (NTS) and area postrema 
in the medullary area of brainstem. The NTS and the area pos -
trema send signals to the rostral centers in the brain. 151,152 The 
brain processes the data and projects descending connections to 
the dorsal vagal complex. The brain also participates in vagovagal 
reflex circuits, which continuously monitor and promptly modify 
responses to changes in the chemical, thermal, and mechanical 
environment in the whole gut (mostly in the esophagus, stomach, 
duodenum, gall bladder, and pancreas). Sensory stimulation 
through this vagal circuit does not appear to reach the level of 
consciousness.151,152
Besides making connections with the NTS and the area pos -
trema in the brainstem, vagal afferents synapse in the dorsal motor 
nucleus of the vagus (DVN) and the nucleus ambiguus, creating 
the dorsal vagal complex. The incoming sensory information is 
integrated and shared with the forebrain and brainstem by the 
dorsal vagal complex. Brain coordination and influences (con -
scious and nonconscious) are translated to the dorsal vagal com -
plex, where the DVN and nucleus ambiguus represent the efferent 
arm of the reflex pathway. It is the final common pathway from the 
brain responsible for precise control of muscle, glandular, and cir-
culatory responses of the GI tract. 149,151,152 Multiple neurotrans-
mitters are involved in the intricate conduction of impulses among 
the neuronal circuits in the dorsal vagal complex. Approximately 
30 are identified and consist of acetylcholine, biogenic amines, 
amino acids, nitric oxide, peptides, etc.151,152
Spinal afferents (splanchnic and pelvic) send sensory impulses 
through the dorsal root ganglia (or prevertebral sympathetic gan -
glia), which are then conducted to the dorsal horn (laminae I, II, 
V , X) of the spinal cord and the dorsal column nuclei. 47,102 The 
dorsal column is thought to play a greater role in nociceptive trans-
mission of messages from the gut than the spinothalamic or spino-
reticular tracts. The conscious perception of pain in the digestive 
tract is largely mediated through spinal afferents. The spinal cord 
modulates the conduction of neural messages (both nociceptive 
and nonnociceptive) to the higher brain. 47 Other somatic and 
visceral sensory stimuli from the vagina, uterus, bladder, colon, 
and rectum are conveyed through the dorsal horn and dorsal col -
umn in the spinal cord as well. Likewise, somatic afferents supply 
sensory input from the muscles of the pelvic floor through the 
pudendal nerve to the sacral region of the spinal cord.10,47,101,102
Brain centers modulate the nociceptive impulses from the 
bowel that are relayed to the dorsal horn of the spinal cord. These 
descending pathways are facilitative or inhibitory or both; based 
on the visceral stimulus; and can alter the perception of pain in 
the digestive tract. 47,119 The neurotransmitters serotonin, nor -
adrenalin, and dopamine are released by these descending path -
ways as they synapse within the spinal cord. 152 Data from the 
brain and descending vagal pathways are conducted to the spinal 
cord and then to the preganglionic neurons in the thoracolumbar  
area (modulates the sympathetic response) and the sacral area 
(modulates the parasympathetic response). Sympathetic or  
parasympathetic output is translated to the neurons in the ENS 
circuitry that can be excitatory or inhibitory to motor neurons, 
gastric or digestive glands, and secretory mechanisms. 47,152
The Alimentary Neuromotor System
The muscles of the GI tract carry out essential functions through-
out the gut that include propulsion, grinding, mixing, absorption, 
storage, and disposal. The submucosal gut wall is composed of 
“self-excitable” smooth muscles that contract in an all-or-none 
manner. It spontaneously responds to stretch and can be indepen-
dent of neural or endocrine control. The interstitial cells of Cajal 
act like a pacemaker and allow propagation of electrical slow 
waves into the circular muscle layer which generates spontaneous 
muscle contraction. Depolarization occurs in an electrical syncy -
tium where action potentials are conducted in three dimensions 
from one smooth muscle fiber to another via gap junctions. 47,152
The sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems modu-
late the ENS, rather than directly controlling the smooth muscles 
of the bowel148 (Fig. 22.3). The smooth muscles of the bowel also 
have their own electromechanical automaticity, which is directly 
modulated by the inhibitory control of the ENS. 45,108 Sympa -
thetic nervous system stimulation tends to promote the storage 
function by enhancing anal tone and inhibiting colonic contrac -
tions, although little perceptible clinical deficit occurs from bilat-
eral sympathectomy.72
Parasympathetic activity enhances colonic motility, and its loss 
is often associated with DWE, including impactions and func -
tional obstructions (e.g., Ogilvie pseudo-obstructive syndrome).72
The ENS and sympathetic postganglionic neurons transmit 
excitatory or inhibitory messages to secretomotor neurons. These 
secretomotor neurons release acetylcholine and vasoactive intesti-
nal polypeptide when there is excitatory activation from paracrine 
stimulation by mucosal and submucosal cells such as enterochro -
maffin cells, mast cells, and other immune and inflammatory cells. 
Acetylcholine and vasoactive intestinal polypeptide are released at 
the neuroepithelial and neurovascular junctions. This promotes 
gut secretion of water, sodium chloride, bicarbonate, and mucus 
drawn from the intestinal glands. In addition, dilatation and  
increase in blood flow occur with release of nitric oxide from vas-
cular endothelium. 6,13,47 Inhibitory influence reduces neuronal 
firing from secretomotor neurons by hyperpolarization of mem -
branes. Sympathetic release of norepinephrine from nerve endings 
of the a2-noradrenergic receptors inhibit secretomotor actuation, 
preventing the release of excitatory neurotransmitters. As a result, 
there is a decrease in secretion of water and electrolytes into the 
lumen and a congruent shunting of blood from the splanchnic to 
systemic circulation.47,88,104
Gastric Motility
Based on its motility pattern the stomach is divided into an upper 
and lower portion. The upper portion (fundus) has sustained, low 
frequency contractions and has a tonic pattern. The lower portion 
(antrum) has intermittent, powerful contractions and has a phasic 
pattern. The fundus acts as a reservoir and accommodates incom-
ing food, which inhibits contraction and allows the stomach to 
stretch without significant increase in pressure. The antrum is a 
mixer that generates propulsive waves that accelerate as food is 
propagated toward the pylorus. The amount and consistency of 
food in the fundus regulates excitatory and inhibitory influence 
and adjustments to volume and pressure.47,71

SECTION 3  Common Clinical Problems
Intestinal Motility
The ENS is designed to control the various patterns of motility in 
the intestinal tract. The interdigestive MMC pattern occurs du r-
ing fasting in the stomach and the small intestine. 47,147 It seems  
to be influenced by the hormone motilin and is responsible for 
removal of waste from the intestinal lumen throughout the fasting 
period.
47,89 When a meal is ingested, the postprandial segmenta -
tion (“mixing”) pattern of motility commences as digestion tran-
spires. The brainstem sends signals that are transmitted to vagal 
efferents, which convert MMC motility to segmentation motility 
with the increase in bulk and nutrients, especially lipids (medium-
chain triglycerides). This subsequently becomes peristaltic motil -
ity, which is propagated through brief segments of intestine at a 
time.
11,47 Peristaltic activity gradually develops into powerful 
contractions sustained through long portions of circular muscle 
along the small and large bowel. These GMCs propel waste 
through the lumen, particularly in the large intestine.
47,64,123
Motility of the Anus, Rectum, and Pelvic Floor
Normal defecation and maintenance of fecal continence entail  
a highly coordinated mechanism that involves the levator ani, 
puborectalis, the external anal sphincter (EAS), and internal anal 
sphincter (IAS) muscles. The pelvic floor includes the levator ani 
and the underlying sheets of muscle, which form a sling around 
the distal rectum. The levator ani, puborectalis, and EAS are skel-
etal muscles that constantly maintain tone supporting pelvic  
organs in place against the forces of gravity and maintain fecal

continence by kinking the rectum toward the pubis.48,58 Simulta-
neous contraction of these muscles prevent the involuntary loss of 
stool and help to maintain the regular pattern of defecation. 40,47
Physiology of Normal Defecation
The colon is a reservoir for food waste until it is convenient for 
elimination. It also acts as a storage device as long as the colonic 
pressure is less than that of the anal sphincter mechanism. Fecal 
elimination occurs when colonic pressure exceeds that of the anal 
sphincter mechanism. Another function of the colon is to reab-
sorb fluids, with up to 30 L/day reabsorbed through the large and 
small bowel walls and typically only 100 mL of water lost in feces. 
The colon also reabsorbs gases, with 90% of the 7 to 10 mL of 
gases produced by intracolonic fermentation daily absorbed rather 
than expelled. The colon additionally provides an environment 
for the growth of bacteria needed to assist in digestion and serves 
to absorb certain bacterial breakdown products as well.
50
The rectum is usually empty until just prior to defecation.  
Perception of external rectal contents and pressures127 is essential for 
signaling voluntary contraction of the anal sphincter. Normal def-
ecation begins with reflexes triggered by rectosigmoid distention 
produced by approximately 200 mL of feces (Fig. 22.4).
110 A rec-
torectal reflex occurs in which the bowel proximal to the distending 
bolus contracts and the bowel wall distally relaxes, serving to propel 
the bolus further downward. Reflex relaxation of the internal 
sphincter and stretching of the puborectalis muscle also occurs, 
which is enhanced by, but does not require, an extrinsic nerve sup-
ply. This relaxation, called the rectoanal inhibitory reflex, correlates

with the urge labeled “the call to stool.”148 One can then volitionally 
contract the levator ani to open the proximal anal canal and relax 
the external sphincter and puborectalis muscles. This allows a 
straighter, shorter, and more open anorectal passage (see Fig. 22.4), 
which permits the bolus to pass. Increasing the intraabdominal 
pressure by squatting and by a Valsalva maneuver assists bolus 
elimination. For 90% of normal individuals only the contents of 
the rectum are expulsed, whereas 10% will clear the entire contents 
of the left or sigmoid colon from the splenic flexure distally.
105
However, one can elect to defer defecation by volitionally con-
tracting the puborectalis muscle and EAS. The reflexive IAS relax-
ation subsequently fades, usually within 15 seconds, and the urge 
resolves until the IAS relaxation is again triggered. The rectal wall 
accommodates to the bolus by decreasing its wall tension with 
time, resulting in less sensory input and less reflex triggering from 
that particular bolus. This continence and reflex process is some -
what analogous to the function of the striated external urethral 
sphincter in volitional control of urinary voiding (see Chapter 21).
The external sphincter generally tenses in response to small 
rectal distentions via a spinal reflex, although reflexive relaxation  
of the external sphincter occurs in the presence of greater disten -
tions.
127 These spinal cord reflexes are centered in the conus 
medullaris and are augmented and modulated by higher cortical 
influences. When cortical control is disrupted, as by SCI, the  
external sphincter reflexes usually persist and allow spontaneous 
defection. During sleep, the colonic activity, anal tone, and protec-
tive responses to abdominal pressure elevations are all decreased, 
while rectal tone increases.
108,148
The “gastrocolonic response” or “gastrocolic reflex” refers  
to the increased colonic activity (GMCs and mass movements) in 
the first 30 to 60 minutes after a meal. This increased colonic 
activity appears to be modulated both by hormonal effects from 
release of peptides from the upper GI tract (gastrin, motilin, and 
cholecystokinin, which increase contractility of colonic smooth 
musculature) and by a reduction in the threshold for spinal cord–
mediated vescicovescical reflexes.
108 Upper GI receptor stimula -
tion also results in increased activity in the colon, possibly due  
to reflexively increased parasympathetic efferent activity to the 
colon. The possibility of a purely ENS-mediated activation exists, 
although the small bowel and colon motor activities do not seem 
to be synchronized. In persons with SCI, the measured increase in 
colonic activity after a meal is blunted as compared with nor -
mals.
108 The gastrocolonic response is often used therapeutically, 
even in SCI patients, to enhance bowel evacuation during this 
30- to 60-minute postprandial time frame.
1 Occasionally certain 
foods can serve as trigger foods that are especially likely to induce 
bowel evacuation shortly after consumption.
The resting anal canal pressure is largely determined by the 
angulation and pressure at the anorectal junction by the puborec-
talis sling and smooth muscle internal sphincter tone. Continence 
is maintained by the anal sphincter mechanism,
94,129 which  
consists of the IAS, EAS, and the puborectalis muscle. 94 Only  
approximately 20% of the anal canal pressure is due to the static 
contraction of the somatically innervated striated EAS.
9 The EAS 
and puborectalis muscle are the only striated skeletal muscles 
whose normal resting state is tonic contraction. These muscles

the situation in nonupright animals such as the cat or dog in 
which it consists of predominately type II fibers).
9 Anal pressure 
can be increased volitionally by contracting the EAS and puborec-
talis muscles. However, maximum volitional squeeze pressures are 
not as high as can be generated reflexively against Valsalva pres -
sure. The EAS is physically larger than the internal sphincter, and 
its contraction is under both reflex and volitional control. The 
volitional control is learned during the course of normal matura -
tion. Normal baseline reflex action of the anorectal mechanism 
allows spontaneous stool elimination.
125 The EAS is innervated  
by the S2 through S4 nerve roots via the pudendal nerve. The 
puborectalis muscle is innervated by direct branches from the  
S1 to S5 roots (see Fig. 22.1).
106 The remarkable degree of learned 
EAS coordination and discriminating rectal sensation allows the 
selective discrete passage of gas while juggling a variable mixture 
of solids, liquids, and gases.
Pathophysiology of Gastrointestinal 
Dysfunction
A whole range of neurologic diseases affecting central, peripheral, 
and intrinsic enteric nervous innervation has been demonstrated to 
result in disorders in various segments of the bowel. These are pre-
dominantly characterized by disturbances in gastroesophageal, 
small or large intestinal motility, and sensation. This chapter con-
centrates on pathologic conditions pertaining to extrinsic neural 
regulation (neurological disorders, degenerative conditions of the 
autonomic nervous system, lesions of the spinal cord, and periph-
eral neuropathies) as well as muscular disorders that influence gas-
trointestinal motility. Symptoms of dysphagia, vomiting, bloating, 
abdominal discomfort and pain, constipation, and incontinence 
have been described in individuals with neurologic ailments.
4,18,19,107 
Exact and detailed pathophysiologic mechanisms of GI dysfunction 
in the various neurologic diseases are not very well understood. 
Recent studies, however, have been able to identify various mecha-
nisms of symptom production that might facilitate therapeutic 
strategies.
The Rome IV classification is a diagnostic system developed to 
standardize the diagnosis and classification of functional gastroin-
testinal disorders (FGIDs), also known as disorders of gut-brain 
interaction. Updated in 2016, Rome IV criteria, reflect advances 
in understanding the pathophysiology, diagnosis, and treatment of 
FGIDs. It can be helpful in classifying chronic or recurrent symp-
toms that arise from neurogenic gastrointestinal dysfunction that 
cannot be explained by structural or biochemical abnormalities.
34
Nausea, Vomiting, Bloating, Early Satiety
The syndrome of nausea, vomiting, bloating, and early satiety in 
the setting of neurologic conditions without mechanical obstruc -
tion can herald motility problems in the GI tract. Neurologic 
dysfunction that affects the inhibitory motor neurons in the ENS 
at any level of the neural axis from the brain, spinal cord, and  
afferent and efferent nerves can lead to dyscoordinated overactiv -
ity of the gastric or intestinal/colonic musculature. When inhibi -
tory motor neurons are inactivated or destroyed by disease, there 
is a continuous, nonsystematic contraction of the circular muscles 
incapable of forward propulsion, causing functional obstruc -
tion.
150,151 This can be manifested as dysphagia, gastroparesis,  
or chronic intestinal/colonic pseudo-obstruction, which might be 
associated with anorexia, abdominal pain, diarrhea, and constipa-
tion57. Inhibitory motor neurons can be affected by denervation  
of central connections autonomic neuropathy, by dysfunction of 
neurons in the myenteric plexus, or from degeneration of smooth 
muscle.
b,7
Abdominal Pain and Discomfort (Box 22.1)
Abdominal pain and discomfort can arise from GI tract disten -
tion and powerful contractions. High-threshold and silent mech-
anoreceptors sense severe distention and intense contractions 
when there is ischemia, injury, inflammation, or obstruction. 
Mechanical and chemical irritants stimulate mechanoreceptors in 
the ENS and translate signals to the brain and spinal cord from 
muscle stretching and contractions.
101,150 Of note, in individuals 
with thoracic SCIs at the 6th thoracic level and above, this can 
result in vague nonlocalized discomfort or autonomic dysreflexia. 
The presence and persistence of ischemia, injury, and inflamma -
tion can elicit abdominal pain from a number of mechanisms: 
Spinal afferent nerves express receptors for inflammatory media -
tors, and the release of bradykinin, adenosine triphosphate,  
adenosine, prostaglandins, leukotrienes, histamine, and mast cell 
proteases heightens sensitivity of these spinal sensory endings. 150 
Amplified levels of 5-HT3 released from hyperactive 5-HT3  
receptors at the vagal and spinal nerve endings facilitate produc -
tion of pain. Excessive induction of serotonergic receptors by 
5-HT3 is due to a defective serotonin transporter.
59,75,150 Central 
sensitization occurs in the spinal cord from constant stimulation 
of the C fibers in the dorsal horn and dorsal column nuclei, and 
neurons become hypersensitive and persistently activated. The 
“wind up” phenomenon develops with the actuation of NMDA 
receptors from the release of glutamate from C fibers. The reactive 
state of dorsal horn and dorsal column neurons becomes a mem -
ory imprint in the spinal cord.
2,3,150 Mechanoreceptors in the gut 
wall can receive a deluge of signals from mechanical irritation. 
This barrage of impulses conducted to the brain can be inter -
preted as nociceptive because of the overwhelming stimula -
tion.
150,152 The descending facilitative modulation of the brain 
through the dorsal horn can promote recognition of ordinary 
sensory input as innocuous. The brain misreads normal noncon-
scious signals relayed from mechanoreceptors as noxious.
119,150

Diarrhea (Box 22.2)
Neurologic dysfunction can present with frequent passage of watery 
stools. Diarrhea occurs when there is overstimulation of secretomotor 
neurons by histamine from inflammatory and immune-mediated 
cells in the mucosa and submucosa and/or vasoactive intestinal pep-
tide and serotonin from mucosal enterochromaffin cells. Moreover, 
these substances influence presynaptic inhibitory receptors to block 
the release of norepinephrine from the postganglionic sympathetic 
fibers that inhibit secretomotor neurons.
149,150

Defecatory Dysfunction: Constipation  
and Fecal Incontinence (Box 22.4)
Neurologic compromise can result in slow transit constipation 
and functional defecation disorders. Slow transit constipation is 
described as a deficiency in the propulsive forces within the colon, 
occurring in the absence of dyssynergic defecation. Functional 
defecation disorders encompass dyssynergic defecation, character-
ized by the paradoxical contraction or insufficient relaxation of 
the pelvic floor musculature during the effort to defecate.
115
Constipation (Box 22.5)
Constipation can be an enigma in neurologic states. Constipation 
can usually be defined as fewer than three bowel movements per 
week. However, constipation is not strictly limited to this defini -
tion because it can include symptoms of abdominal discomfort, 
bloating, distention, increased straining, sensation of incomplete 
emptying and anal blockage, and need for manual removal of 
stool.
12 Constipation related to NBD is often due a number  
of factors that are caused directly by the neurologic dysfunction 
and other indirect factors, such as decreased mobility, altered  
appetite, altered fluid intake, changes to lifestyle routine, medica-
tion effects, and psychological issues.
Constipation effects of NBD results from impaired or absent 
muscle and sensory function that affects defecation. In supraconal 
lesions (above lumbar level 1), rectal emptying becomes inadequate 
due to poor propulsive forces and/or increased resistance. This  
is typically due to both high anal resting pressures and recto-
sphincteric dyssynergia with paradoxic contraction or incomplete 
pelvic floor relaxation during defecation.
77,137,139 Infraconal lesions  
(at lumbar level 1 and below) will present with the opposite picture: 
weak or absent sphincter tone, and atonic pelvic floor muscles. 
Both of these conditions result in obstructed defecation and are not 
uncommonly associated with prolonged colonic transit.
77,137,139
Infrequent, incomplete emptying of hard stools is a result of a 
decrease in secretion of water and electrolytes into the lumen due 
to reduced excitation of the secremotor neurons in the ENS or 
prolonged stool transit time and overabsorption of water. Norepi-
nephrine released by sympathetic stimulation inhibits the firing of 
secretomotor neurons by hyperpolarization. Release of excitatory 
neurotransmitters is reduced in the secretory epithelium, decreas-
ing the secretion of water and electrolytes.
150,152 Lack of rectal 
sensation and decreased urge to defecate is associated with consti-
pation in various conditions that present with lesions of the brain, 
spinal cord, sacral nerves, and hypogastric and pudendal nerves.

Reflexic Supraconal Neurogenic  
Bowel Dysfunction
Any destructive CNS process above the conus, from SCI to demen-
tia, can lead to supraconal NBD pattern. Spinal cortical sensory 
pathway deficits lead to decreased ability to sense the urge to defe-
cate. However, most persons with SCI sense a vague discomfort 
when excessive rectal or colonic distention occurs. It has been  
reported that 43% have chronic complaints of vague abdominal 
distention discomfort that eases with bowel evacuation.
92,132 These 
sensations might be mediated by autonomic nervous system affer-
ent fibers bypassing the zone of SCI via the paraspinal sympathetic 
chain or by means of vagal parasympathetic afferents.
Anal sensation, rectal compliance, sphincter tone and func -
tion, and pelvic floor relaxation have been studied in SCI subjects 
using anorectal physiologic testing to assess defecation. Resting 
sphincter tone has been found to be normal or exaggerated com -
pared with normal controls.
80,93 Rectal sensation was impaired in 
complete SCI lesions and less impaired in incomplete lesions.93,138 
Voluntary anal squeeze was present in most patients with motor 
incomplete lesions and absent in all patients with motor complete 
lesions.
138 Rectal sphincter dyssynergia and the absence of sphinc-
ter and pelvic floor relaxation were observed during simulated 
defecation in supraconal lesions,
31,93,138 most likely contributing 
to significant difficulty with defecation. 139 In addition, increased 
rectal tone and decreased rectal compliance91 with associated anal 
resistance with defecation and the attenuation of EAS contraction 
with increased intraabdominal pressure and during rectal disten-
tion also contribute to difficulty with defecation.
80,93,139 This was 
demonstrated in both acute and chronic supraconal lesions. 80
SCI subjects with complete supraconal lesions were studied 
during the acute phase (5 to 21 days) after SCI. These same  
patients were reevaluated 6 to 14 months later. T otal GI transit 
time was longer during the acute rather than the chronic phase. 
The supraconal neurogenic colon tended to have slower transit 
throughout the colon with less severe rectosigmoid dysfunc-
tion.
81 Patients with supraconal NBD have spared reflex arc 
control of the rectosigmoid and pelvic floor. Internal sphincter 
relaxation upon rectal distention occurs in persons with SCI as 
well as in neurologically intact persons. Sufficient rectal disten -
tion might cause the external sphincter to completely relax,  
resulting in expulsion of the fecal bolus. Rectal sphincter dys -
synergia does not necessarily correlate with bladder sphincter 
dyssynergia, but it often results in DWE.
106 The protective vesi-
corectal reflex, in which the external sphincter pressure increases 
in response to increased intraabdominal pressure, is usually intact 
(Table 22.1).
9 Patients with supraconal NBD also have normal or 
increased anal sphincter tone, intact anocutaneous (or anal wink) 
and bulbocavernosus reflexes,
129 a palpable puborectalis muscle 
sling, and normal anal verge appearance (Fig. 22.5).

Polyneuropathy, conus medullaris or cauda equina lesions, pelvic

the anal sphincter mechanism. Anorectal physiologic studies show

reduced or absent in the infraconal NBD. 80,139 These conditions 
can also produce sympathetic and parasympathetic innervation 
deficits. If an isolated pudendal nerve insult has occurred, colonic 
transit times are normal and FI predominates. Distal colonic slug-

through the rectosigmoid segments due to the lack of direct inner-
vation from the conus.

Gastrointestinal Dysfunction in  
Common Neurologic Disorders
Brain Disorders
Strokes (ischemic or hemorrhagic), cerebral trauma, mass, infec -
tion, or other lesions cause a whole range of neurologic damage 
and complications to many areas of the brain. They commonly 
present with dysphagia, delays in gastric emptying and ileus  
(intestinal pseudo-obstruction), constipation, and FI. Brainstem 
or cranial nerve involvement often causes dysphagia. Studies  
report dysphagia occurs in approximately 45% of patients with 
stroke in the acute phase. Cerebral edema and high intracranial 
pressure play a role in GI ulceration, gastroparesis, or ileus 
through unknown mechanisms. Depending on the severity of the 
cerebral or brainstem involvement, dysphagia and ileus progres -
sively improve. Approximately 23% of stroke patients were shown 
to have FI in stroke research, and constipation has been observed 
to be very common in clinical practice. 4,19,91,107
Parkinson Disease and Parkinson Plus Diseases
Parkinson and Parkinson-plus diseases (multiple system atrophy, 
progressive supranuclear palsy, corticobasal degeneration) are  
neurodegenerative diseases that affect various parts of the nervous 
system, including the cerebral cortex, basal ganglia, brainstem, 
cerebellum, and spinal cord. They are characterized by dementia, 
muscle rigidity, bradykinesia, resting tremor, dystonia, shuffling 
gait, and dysphagia. They also have dysautonomia, which is mani-
fested as orthostatic hypotension, sexual dysfunction, gastric dys -
motility, and constipation. Up to 50% of people with Parkinson 
disease have been found to have dysphagia and problems with 
defecation. In a study done on 98 people, 52.1% had dysphagia, 
28.7% had constipation, and 65% had problems with defecation. 
These GI problems can be severe enough to compromise nutrition 
and cause severe morbidity.26,39,82,100,109,113
Multiple Sclerosis
Multiple sclerosis is a demyelinating disease caused by an autoim-
mune process that involves different regions of the brain and 
spinal cord. Initial presentations can be any neurologic sign  
or symptom, which can consist of paresis, spasticity, ataxia, pares-
thesias, visual problems, cognitive deficits, mood disorders, dys -
phagia, gut dysmotility, urinary incontinence or retention, FI, 
diarrhea, or constipation. In a study done on patients with mul -
tiple sclerosis, 68% were reported to have GI problems, with 43% 
having constipation and 53% having FI. The bowel and bladder 
problems in these diseases are believed to be due to supraspinal or 
spinal lesions associated with autonomic sympathetic and para -
sympathetic system impairment.19,26,121,144,145
Spinal Cord Injuries and Disorders
T rauma, masses, infection, hemorrhage, or ischemia to the spinal 
cord causing tetraplegia or paraplegia primarily affects colonic 
motility and perineal and anorectal sensation and function in the 
acute and chronic phases. However, spinal cord–related upper GI 
tract problems have been more frequently recognized. Depending 
on the level of the injury, brain and autonomic modulation of the 
ENS is impaired. The bowel dysfunction is principally one of 
motility rather than secretion or absorption. It has been demon -
strated that the upper digestive tract is more involved in people 
with tetraplegia than with paraplegia and is characterized by gas -
troparesis, impaired gastric emptying, and delayed GI transit 
times. Dysphagia is common in people with cervical cord lesions 
in the acute phase and often eventually resolves. Gastric and duo-
denal ulcerations are prevalent in people with myelopathy. Across 
all levels of injury, there is a loss of voluntary control over bowel 
movements resulting in constipation and FI. Supraconal lesions 
present with preserved rectoanal reflexes and spastic sphincters. 
Infraconal lesions display flaccidity of the sphincters with loss  
of reflexes.
Peripheral Neuropathy
There are multiple causes of peripheral neuropathy including 
metabolic, viral, traumatic, toxic, metastatic, and genetic. Sensory 
and motor deficits are associated with autonomic involvement. 
Disturbances in GI transit are most often caused by peripheral 
neuropathies. Motility problems might result in diarrhea (from 
bacterial overgrowth) or constipation. Persons with diabetes are 
well known to develop gastroparesis and decreased intestinal  
motility primarily from autonomic dysfunction. In paraneoplastic 
syndromes in small cell carcinoma of the lung or pulmonary carci-
noid, immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibody against enteric neurons 
has been found to be responsible for esophageal dysmotility, gas -
troparesis, and constipation. Injury to the pudendal nerve (from 
childbirth or protracted straining), S2, S3, or S4 nerve root lesions, 
or cauda equina underlies FI and abnormal defecation.
Comprehensive Evaluation
History
Recognition of all aspects of impairment and disability that limit a 
person’s ability to maintain continence and volitionally defecate 
must be assessed. GI history should not only review for cardinal 
symptoms but should also address the patient’s general neuromus-
cular and GI function (Box 22.7). A detailed review of the patient’s 
bowel program includes an assessment of fluids, diet, activity, 
medications, and aspects of bowel care. It is imperative to deter -
mine defecation frequency, stool consistency (using the Bristol 
Stool Scale, Fig. 22.6),85 stool amount per bowel movement, typical

For a quick, standardized, valid method of collecting informa-

Physical Examination
The physical examination should include the GI system and the 
associated parts of the musculoskeletal and nervous systems  
required for adequate management of the bowel program  
(Box 22.8). The examination should be completed at the initial 
assessment and then annually for SCI.
129 The purpose of the  
examination is to detect functional changes, screen for complica -
tions, and identify any new masses or lesions.
The abdomen should be inspected for distention, hernias, and 
other abnormalities. Careful assessment for the presence or absence 
of bowel sounds should be done. Percussion and auscultation 
should precede palpation for masses and tenderness. With the  
abdomen relaxed, the examiner palpates the abdomen in all quad-
rants. Palpable hard stool in the colon should be noted and should 
not be present on the right side of the abdomen (ascending colon). 
Gastroparesis, intestinal, and colonic pseudo-obstruction present 
with an abdomen that is tender, distended, and tympanic with 
hypoactive bowel sounds.
57 This can be accompanied by autonomic 
dysreflexia in people with SCI. The patient may show signs of mal-
nutrition and dehydration, including loss of weight, pale skin, dry 
mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, orthostatic hypotension, and 
tachycardia.
The rehabilitation evaluation should be interdisciplinary and a 
process driven by an updated problem list, changes in patient 
function, alterations in resources, and updated patient-centered 
goals. It should include an assessment not only for colon and 
pelvic floor dysfunction, but also for impairments of other organs 
or systems that could affect rehabilitative strategies to make bowel 
care independent or prevent unplanned bowel movements.
Physical examination continues with inspection of the anus.  
A patulous, gaping orifice suggests a history of overdistention and 
trauma by a previous regimen. A normal anal-buttock contour 
(see Fig. 22.5) suggests an intact EAS muscle mass, whereas its 
loss results in a flattened, fanned-out “scalloped” appearing anal 
region. The patient should perform a Valsalva maneuver while the 
examiner observes the anus and perineum for excessive descent.

Perianal cutaneous sharp stimulation normally results in an exter-
nally visible anal sphincter reflexive contraction. This is the ano-
cutaneous reflex, mediated by the inferior hemorrhoidal branch of 
the pudendal nerve (S2 to S5). Sensation to light touch and pin -
prick is tested at the same time. If deep anal pressure is perceived 
in SCI, this denotes incompleteness of the SCI on the Interna -

Injury examination.
69 The tone and voluntary squeeze strength of 
the EAS and tone of the IAS should be assessed. SCI is noted to 
be American Spinal Injury Association Impairment Scale C (AIS C) 
if this is present,
69 implying better prognosis for motor function. 
Integrity of the pelvic floor muscles can be examined by the abil -
ity to contract and relax. The length of the anus, where pressure is 
sensed, is normally 2.5 to 4.5 cm. The point where pressure  
decreases marks the anorectal junction. Along the posterior wall, 
1.5 to 2.5 cm from the anal verge, the puborectalis muscular sling 
can be palpated as a ridge that will push the finger forward toward 
the pubis as the subject resists defecation. No palpable ridge or 
push suggests puborectalis atrophy or dysfunction. A shortened 
length of anal pressure zone suggests EAS muscle atrophy. With 
the examiner’s finger in place, the bulbocavernosus reflex can be 
elicited by rapidly tapping or squeezing the clitoris or glans penis. 
The response can be delayed up to a few seconds in pathologic 
conditions. Insertion of the finger in the anal canal occasionally 
triggers IAS relaxation but more often triggers a tightening EAS 
contraction that is efferently equivalent to the bulbocavernosus 
reflex. The bulbocavernosus reflex (and the anocutaneous reflex) 
is weak or absent when in spinal shock or in infraconal lesions. 
Ask the patient to volitionally squeeze the anus before removing 
the finger (“resist defecation”) to check for volitional EAS and 
puborectalis tone and control. In addition, ask the patient to bear 
down and relax alternately to evaluate contraction, relaxation, and 
coordination of the pelvic muscles.
Diagnostic Testing
Additional objective laboratory testing can be helpful when the 
cause of FI or DWE is obscure, the history appears doubtful, 
conservative interventions fail, or surgical interventions are con -
templated. Table 22.2 lists some of the many tests available.
Radiographic testing complements the physical examination 
(Box 22.9). A flat plate radiograph with or without an upright of 
the abdomen can be helpful to evaluate for the degree of fecal 
loading, rule out impaction, megacolon, obstruction, and a per -
forated viscus.
12,15 If further indicated, a computed tomography 
(CT) scan of the abdomen is a reliable, specific, and sensitive 
method in assessing detailed structural issues like small or large 
intestinal obstructions and other lesions.
12,15

Colon transit testing requires swallowing radiopaque markers 
that are followed by abdominal radiographs as they pass through 
the various parts of the colon. Colon transit time is measured by 
counting the passage of markers through the right, transverse, and 
rectosigmoid segments of the colon.
12,141 Studies completed on 
people with NBD due to SCI have shown that transit time is pri-
marily delayed in the left colon and rectum, but may be panco -
lonic.35,77,137 The Wireless Motility Capsule has been shown to  
be an essential method for measuring GI transit time for NBD 
with the crucial advantage of measuring segmental gastric, small 
intestinal, and colonic transit time all with one test compared with 
conventional testing done with separate tests, on different days.
146
For people who have neurologic issues with associated consti -
pation/FI and who have present but impaired sensation and/or 
motor function, the anal rectal manometry test is helpful in char-
acterizing a defecatory disorder (Fig. 22.9).
12,116,141 Supraconal 
NBD may present as dyssynergia (paradoxic contraction of the 
rectal sphincter) and/or failure to relax pelvic floor muscles during 
simulated defecation, with less than 20% decrease in anal canal 
pressures.
77,139 It may be associated with inadequate defecatory 
propulsion due to inadequate increase in rectal or intraabdominal 
pressure during simulated defecation.
139 Infraconal injuries will 
present alternatively with decreased rectal reflexes and decreased 
resting and volitional sphincter pressures.
77,139
Anorectal manometry is typically accompanied by balloon ex-
pulsion testing (BET), which involves evacuation of a lubricated 
balloon-tipped catheter. BET is known to have high specificity for 
dyssynergia. Balloon expulsion time is abnormal if greater than  
1 minute.
12,116,141 BET has been used to further evaluate defeca -
tory disorders in NBD.77,93,139
EMG activity can be recorded with electrodes applied to bilat-
eral areas of the rectum to augment anorectal and pelvic floor 
testing.
12,116,141 It is another measure used to detect dyssynergia or 
characterize defecation dysfunction in NBD. It is often per-
formed with pudendal nerve latency testing, which assists with 
identifying peripheral pudendal nerve injury.
46,77,139,143
Defecography, using fluoroscopy or magnetic resonance imag-
ing, is a dynamic assessment of the anorectum and pelvic floor 
muscles before, during, and after attempted defecation of barium 
paste applied in the rectum. The puborectalis muscle can be  
assessed for contraction (dyssynergia) and relaxation (normal) 
during simulated defecation by indirectly measuring the angle 
between the axis of the rectum and anal canal. Defecography is 
most useful for evaluation of structural causes of outlet dysfunc -
tion like rectal prolapse, rectocele, and enterocele.
12,116,141
Management
Management of Nausea, Vomiting,  
Bloating, and Early Satiety
Nausea, vomiting, bloating, and early satiety may indicate gastro-
paresis; a small intestinal or colonic pseudo-obstruction in people 
with neurologic disease.
57 Acute or chronic episodes of subocclu -
sion, characterized by derangement of gut propulsive motility re -
sembling mechanical obstruction in the absence of any obstructive 
process, can occur. There may be findings of distended bowel loops 
and air-fluid levels requiring GI decompression with nasogastric  
or rectal tubes. The patient may be placed on bowel rest and, to 
reduce gut irritation, consume nothing by mouth. Nutritional, 
fluid, and electrolyte support must be provided with intravenous 
infusion. Drugs such as opiates, anticholinergics, tricyclics, antihis-
tamines, calcium-channel blockers, and phenothiazines (all of 
which profoundly diminish motility) must be minimized or dis -
continued. Once decompression is achieved, feeding is slowly  
advanced to a general diet. Prokinetic agents such as metoclo -
pramide and domperidone (dopamine antagonists) and erythro -
mycin (macrolide antibiotic, motilin receptor agonist) have  
been found to be beneficial in facilitating gastric emptying and

increase in bacteria or other pathogens in the upper GI tract (stom-
ach, duodenum, jejunum, and proximal ileum), where there is 
normally low amounts of bacteria.
117 There are many causes of 
bacterial overgrowth syndrome, but with neurologic disease, it is 
most likely due to upper GI tract dysmotility leading to decreased 
clearance of unwanted bacteria and undigested substances.
16  
T o diagnose this condition, the glucose hydrogen breath test is the 
simplest to perform. Levels of exhaled hydrogen to 20 ppm indicate 
a positive test. This test is 82% sensitive and 62.5% specific.
44 T reat-
ment with rifaximin (550 mg two to three times per day) is recom-
mended.42 A combination of ciprofloxacin and metronidazole has 
also been shown to be effective.7,20
Diarrhea in neurologic disease is commonly due to severe con-
stipation. When solid stools are not passed regularly, they become 
very hard and are difficult to pass, causing a blockage of waterier

shown in a Cochrane Review to reduce duration of illness with 
diarrhea lasting less than 4 days and decrease in stool frequency.

Management of Defecation Dysfunction: 
Constipation and Fecal Incontinence
Management of NBD involves a pyramidal stepwise method, 
which commences with a personalized bowel program and con -
servative treatment composed of minimizing constipating medi -
cations, good intake of fluids and fiber, oral laxatives, and rectal 
medications; and mechanical strategies for bowel evacuation  
(Fig. 22.10).
36,54,76
A bowel program is a comprehensive individualized patient-
centered treatment plan focused on preventing incontinence, 
achieving effective and efficient colonic evacuation, and prevent -
ing the complications of NBD. The subcomponents of a bowel 
program address diet, fluids, exercise, medications, and scheduled 
bowel care. Bowel care is the individually developed and pre -
scribed procedure for defecation that is carried out by the patient 
or attendant.
67,129
NBD results in problems with fecal storage and elimination. 
Inability to volitionally inhibit spontaneous defecations leads to 
incontinence while the inability to adequately empty leads to 
constipation and impactions. Paradoxically, social continence is 
achieved by impaction and can result in diarrhea and inconti -
nence. Providing adequate timely emptying must be combined 
with the inhibition of spontaneous defecations except at desired 
times.
130 The individual life roles of the patient that are achieved 
after completing the acute rehabilitation process determine the 
bowel program and bowel care schedule. The demands of life  
activities, the duration of bowel care, attendant care options, and 
the needs of other members of the household should all be con -
sidered when designing the bowel program and the process of 
bowel care.
70,129
Goals of the Bowel Program
It is essential to outline the goals of the bowel program as a per -
son-centered plan for each patient ( Box 22.10). Each person  
responds differently to various techniques and medications used 
to treat constipation and FI. Regular bowel emptying is recom -
mended as the primary means for enhancing both elimination 
and decreasing incontinence between stooling. The goals of the 
bowel program should include
12,117:
 1. R egular, planned passage of stool on a daily or every other day 
basis (at least 3 times per week)
 2. A dequate stool outputs per bowel movement (moderate-large 
stools)
 3. Bo wel evacuation at a consistent time of day (AM or PM)
 4. Complete emptying of the r ectal vault with every bowel care 
session
 5. S tools that are soft, formed, and bulky

Fully appreciating an individual’s premorbid “normal” bowel 
function is important in the planning and goal setting for a new 
neurogenic bowel program. A wide range of “normal” bowel pat-
terns exist. Ninety-five percent have a frequency of between three 
times per day and three times per week. Stool consistencies vary 
from liquid to pudding, pasty, semisolid, soft formed, medium 
formed, and hard formed.67,70
The choice of the frequency and specific timing of bowel care 
to induce adequate colonic emptying can be based on previous 
elimination patterns. However, it is best to plan for every day  
or every other day bowel movements to avoid episodes of FI or 
impaction. Waiting more than 2 days to have a bowel movement 
increases episodes of constipation and incomplete emptying of 
the rectum. Establishing a consistent schedule for bowel care  
facilitates predictability and planning. Ensuring complete empty-
ing of the rectum decreases incontinence or stooling during the 
course of the day. Incontinence is reduced by less stool accumula-
tion because stool is not presented to the rectum between desired 
defecation times. Adequate emptying is accomplished by making 
stools easier to propel by softening, adding bowel stimulant 
medication if needed, and triggering the defecatory reflex at con -
sistent desired times to promote habituation. If conal and local 
rectal reflex methods do not deliver stool, manual evacuation or 
various irrigating techniques are effective.67,70,130
Bowel programs and techniques for bowel care training should 
begin during inpatient rehabilitation and then be monitored, 
maintained, or modified at outpatient visits. Some patients need 
attendants who are well trained to help with bowel care. 129 The 
burden of care for persons with neurogenic bowel is much higher 
if continence is not achieved or if bowel care evacuation times are 
excessive. It is crucial that the patient takes a decisive leadership 
role in designing a bowel program that includes a convenient 
bowel care schedule. Educating patients about their altered neu -
rogenic bowel physiology empowers them with options and tech-
niques to construct a bowel care regimen compatible with their 
daily routine. Making them independent with their bowel care is 
an important aspect of the overall rehabilitation process. 67,70,129
Dietary Considerations
Food and fluid choices are important when colonic transit time is 
prolonged, such as in SCI where the transit time is often 96 hours 
versus the 30 hours typically found in normal subjects. The main 
goal of dietary choices is to achieve soft, well-formed, bulky 
stools. Excessive fluid resorption can result in stool hardening and 
subsequent constipation. Gases and liquids are propelled by the 
colon 30 to 100 times faster than solids. Stools that have lost their 
plasticity might not be kneaded and folded properly by the haus -
tra, impeding the transit time.
Food fiber has been used to maintain a more fluid content. 
Fiber increases stool bulk and plasticity, especially in the more 
physically coarse forms, which also tend to decrease colonic pres -
sures.153 Control of excessive stool hardness requires higher-fiber 
foods in preference to lower-residual foods. However, it is impor-
tant to remember that sufficient intake of fluids (preferably water) 
is imperative when taking a high-fiber diet. The Institute of 
Medicine recommends daily fluid intake of 3 L for males and  
2.2 L for females.111 Otherwise, a high-fiber diet with insufficient 
intake of fluids can promote constipation. It should be remem -
bered that highly caffeinated fluids such as coffee, tea, and energy 
drinks can lead to dehydration from diuresis.
A diet that contains at least 38 g for males and 25 g for females 
of fiber daily is recommended (15 g initially and gradually  
increase as tolerated).62,130 Aside from fiber from vegetables, fruits, 
and grains, processed fiber products can be used such as psyllium 
(Metamucil, Fiberall), calcium polycarbophil (Fibercon), and 
methylcellulose (Citrucel). One study showed that taking psyl -
lium for 8 weeks increased the number of stools per week and 
stool weight in patients with Parkinson disease without affecting 
colonic transit time. A case series study proposes that increase in 
fiber in individuals with SCI may not improve, but impair bowel 
function compared with people with normal bowels. 17,76 The  
effects of fiber intake on stool consistency, frequency, and efficacy 
of evacuation should be evaluated in each individual patient,  
and fiber intake should be titrated accordingly. High pressures 
involved in moving solid feces probably contribute to the 90% 
incidence of hemorrhoids in Americans and to premature diver -
ticula formation and hemorrhoidal complications in more than 
70% of SCI patients.129 Constant straining at stool can contribute 
to peripheral neuropathic deficits in the anal sphincter muscula -
ture.129 Softer stools, from a higher-fiber diet, might help to  
reduce these complications.
Treatment Approaches and Rationale
T o select the best approach, the supraconal NBD must be delin-
eated from the infraconal NBD. Brain disease and spinal cord 
disorders above the conus present with reflexic NBD. Peripheral 
neuropathy and spinal cord disorders below the conus present 
with hyporeflexic NBD. Colonic transit time and fecal elimina -
tion are enhanced by softer stool. However, if the stool becomes 
too liquid, the protective angle provided by the puborectalis  
becomes less effective, and greater EAS pressures are required to 
maintain continence. Neurogenic bowel resting anal pressures are 
usually normal to slightly decreased, but patients are unable to 
develop the protective increases in EAS tone needed to control 
more liquid stool.105 Some degree of stool firmness must be toler-
ated to prevent incontinence. T o avoid incontinence upon strain-
ing, more firmness (medium formed) is required for the weaker 
anal sphincter mechanism of infraconal NBD than for supraconal 
NBD (semi formed to soft formed).
Fiber more consistently softens stool but also adds bulk. 
Bulkier stools can help to prevent liquefaction of stool and main-
tain continence for supraconal NBD. Fiber maintains consistency 
and ease of propulsion and prevents left colonic compaction with 
constipation. Adequate fluid intake is required for fiber to be 
helpful; otherwise, fiber can contribute to constipation. 62
Goals of the Bowel Program
• Regular, planned passage of stool on a daily or every other day basis  
(at least 3x per week)
• Adequate stool outputs per bowel movement (moderate-large stools)
• Bowel evacuation at a consistent time of day (AM or PM)
• Complete emptying of the rectal vault with every bowel care session
• Stools that are soft, formed, and bulky
• Completing the bowel care within half an hour (at most within 1 hour)
• No episodes of fecal incontinence or unplanned bowel movements

For a bowel program to be successful, a combination of oral 
and rectal medications and bowel evacuation techniques might be 
required to facilitate more consistent, regular bowel emptying. 
Stool must effectively pass through the entire length of the colon 
(approximately 5 feet) for a successful bowel movement to occur. 
Careful consideration of how medications affect stool consistency 
is critical to avoid issues with FI.
Rapid advancement of stool to the rectum, triggering defeca -
tion, may be due more to GMC and mass movements than with 
the slow accumulation of sufficient rectal stool to trigger reflex 
defecation. The GMC might be what is actually habituated. 131 
Choosing long intervals between elimination allows more fluid 
reabsorption, resulting in harder stools, which can worsen DWE. 
Because 95% of unimpaired persons defecate three or more times 
per week, choosing a frequency of at least every other day would 
seem more physiologic and less likely to contribute to constipa -
tion. One study of well-managed SCI patients found frequencies 
of bowel care to be chosen as daily by 24%, every other day by 
46%, and more often than three times a week by 85%. The desire 
to avoid the unpleasant task of stool elimination leads some to 
elect longer time intervals between bowel care sessions. This car -
ries the risk for impaction or sphincter damage caused by rectal 
distention by larger-caliber, harder stools.
One approach to initiating neurogenic bowel training is out -
lined in Box 22.11. Each step is added only after a 7-day consis -
tent trial of the previous step has been ineffective. In this stepwise 
approach, obtaining elimination at the desired time is emphasized 
as the first step and usually precedes development of complete 
continence by several weeks. This regimen is designed to enhance 
responsiveness and emptying at the habituated time with less 
stool presentation between bowel care sessions.
Management of Reflexic Supraconal  
Neurogenic Bowel Dysfunction
In supraconal NBD, the defecatory reflex is intact, so that trigger-
ing of defecation can be accomplished by digital stimulation, 
rectal stimulant medications, enemas, or electrical stimulation. All 
of these cause reflex relaxations of the IAS and, if strong enough, 
can reflexively relax the EAS as well. This activates preserved ano-
rectal colonic reflexes that increase motility of the left colon and 
help to eliminate any stool that is present. 74,76 The GMC and 
mass movement associated with the “call to stool” for many intact 
persons often occurs at consistent times, which can be trainable.
Digital rectal stimulation is done by inserting a gloved, lubri -
cated finger into the rectum and stimulating the rectal wall with 
gentle circular movements. This should be done for 20 seconds 
and repeated every 5 to 10 minutes until the bowel care is com -
pleted and the rectal vault is emptied.
Management of Hyporeflexic Infraconal 
Neurogenic Bowel Dysfunction
In infraconal NBD, the bowel’s reflex branches to and from the 
conus are interrupted and the most effective way of completely 
emptying the rectum is manual disimpaction with Valsalva and 
gravity. The process can be enhanced with lubrications, enemas, 
and mineral oil or with the use of cleansing enemas (water, soap -
suds, mineral oil, milk of molasses) that are done daily or twice a 
day. Sphincter and pelvic floor muscle tone is lost, increasing the 
likelihood of FI. Consequently, it is imperative to keep the stools 
well-formed and bulky (i.e., BSS4) and to empty the rectal vault 
more regularly. Use of oral medications as outlined for the supra-
conal NBD might likewise be warranted if stool is not efficiently 
reaching the rectum in a timely manner.
Intrinsic loss of the ENS in any segment, or surgical reanasto -
mosis, can result in loss of the rectoanal inhibitory reflex, causing 
DWE. Oral laxative abuse can cause dysfunction of the ENS. If 
bowel training is not accomplishing defecation at the desired 
times or if repeated involuntary incontinence occurs, further  
diagnostic evaluation might be indicated (see Table 22.2).
Rectal Medications
There are a variety of rectally administered medications that are 
used to trigger and sustain reflex defecation. These are usually 
inserted into the rectum to start bowel care expulsion and trigger 
gas and stool flow in as soon as a minute or as long as 30 minutes 
after liquification. Digital stimulation is then initiated to sustain 
the process, detect stool for manual disimpaction, or confirm the 
rectum is clear. The rationale for this prescription is to use the 
least irritating and most easily inserted and retained medication. 
Typically used suppositories include glycerin vegetable oil–based 
bisacodyl (Dulcolax) and polyethylene glycol–based (PGB)  
bisacodyl (Magic Bullet). Options for mini-enema–triggered 
bowel care include small-volume phosphor-soda enema, bisacodyl 
enema, and docusate mini-enema (Enemeez, formerly Theravac). 
Clinical efficacy studies have been conducted in attempts to  
measure efficiency and effectiveness of bowel care using various  
rectally administered triggering medications. In a randomized 
blinded study, hydrogenated vegetable oil–based (HVB) bisacodyl 
suppositories, PGB suppositories, and the docusate (Theravac) 
mini-enema were compared. Subjects with supraconal NBD were 
studied using the Events and Intervals of Bowel Care. 60 Bowel 
care trials with PGB bisacodyl suppositories showed an average 
time to defection of approximately 22 minutes and a total bowel 
care time of 50 minutes. This was much shorter than the average 
time to defection of 40 minutes and total bowel care time of  
85 minutes observed with HVB bisacodyl suppository trials.  
Protocol for Progressive Steps in Bowel 
Habituation Program
 1. Perform bowel cleanout if stool is present in the rectal vault or palpable 
proximal to the descending colon by multiple enemas or oral cathartic.
 2. Titrate to soft stool consistency with diet and bulking agents (fiber) and 
stool softeners (docusate).
 3. Trigger defecation with a bisacodyl suppository or by digital stimulation  
20 to 30 minutes after a meal; 10 minutes later, have the patient attempt 
defecation on the toilet, limited to ,40 minutes and relieving skin pressure 
every 10 minutes.
 4. If defecation is not initiated, a trial of a bisacodyl suppository per rectum is 
initiated.
 5. Digital stimulation. Start 15 minutes after suppository placement and repeat 
every 5 minutes.
 6. Timed oral medications. Administer docusate (Colace) or senna–docusate 
sodium (Peri-Colace), senna (Senokot), or bisacodyl (Dulcolax) tablets timed 
approximately 6 to 8 hours prior (adjust accordingly) so that bowel move -
ment would otherwise result 30 minutes to 1 hour after anticipated trig -
gered bowel timing.
 7. If defecation occurs in ,10 minutes after suppository insertion, transition 
to digital stimulation technique only. Once the patient is well habituated, 
straining alone may rarely trigger defecations at a desired time.

The use of mini-enemas had similar efficiency as PGB supposito-
ries. Theravac has since been replaced by Enemeez. Results vary in 
individual patients and the use of digital stimulation alone is suf -

In supraconal NBD, digital stimulation to induce defecatory 
reflexes should be favored over manual disimpaction because the 
latter can easily result in inadvertent overstretching of the insen -
sate and more delicate anal mechanisms of the neurogenic bowel. 
Local rectal stimulant suppositories and mini-enemas with bisaco-
dyl or glycerin do not carry the same risk as oral stimulant medi -
cations and do not appear to lead to chronic inflammatory 
changes of the rectal mucosa.
Oral Medications for Bowel Care
When digital rectal stimulation, rectal medications, and rectal 
evacuation of stool are not sufficient to achieve the goals of the 
bowel program, oral medications are indicated ( Table 22.3 ). 
Changes in medication with trials at active doses require at least  
2 weeks or 4 bowel care sessions, at a minimum, to determine  
if a clinical response has started. Colon stimulants such as senna 
(Senokot) and bisacodyl (Dulcolax) activate colonic movement and 
increase fluid and electrolyte secretion in the GI tract. Osmotic 
laxatives promote water retention in the colon lumen. Osmotic 
laxatives that are commonly used can be classified into the follow-
ing: (1) PGB solutions (e.g., Miralax), (2) nonabsorbable carbohy-
drates (e.g., lactulose [Cephulac]), (3) magnesium-containing  
solutions (milk of Magnesia, magnesium citrate), and (4) sodium 
phosphate–based products and stool softeners such as docusate 
(Colace). Determining the most appropriate oral medication or 
combinations thereof is usually done by trial and error based on 
type, dosage, quantity, and duration of efficacy. Constantly being 
aware of the goals of the bowel program assists with titration, revi-
sion, and timing of intake of oral medications. Approaches that 
appear effective initially need longer-term studies to verify their 
continued benefits due to a high incidence of late GI problems  
reported in an initially successfully managed SCI population.67
Newer Oral Medications to Treat Constipation
Secretagogues. Secretagogues promote colonic transit and 
intestinal secretion by either affecting chloride channels or gua -
nylate cyclase receptors.
Lubiprostone. Lubiprostone (Amitiza) increases intestinal 
fluid secretion by activating type 2 chloride channels, which pro -
motes GI motility. This enhances intestinal and colonic transit 
and helps with passage of stool. It affects prostaglandin E recep -
tors, which facilitate gastric and colonic muscle contraction and 
motility.37,98
Linaclotide. Linaclotide (Linzess) is a 14-amino peptide agonist 
of guanylate cyclase-C (GC-C) receptor located on the luminal 
surface of intestinal epithelial cells. It increases cyclic guanosine  
monophosphate (cGMP), which triggers a signal transduction cas-
cade activating the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regu-
lator. This results in secretion of fluid into the lumen and promotes 
intestinal transit. It is further shown to relieve abdominal pain  
by activating GC-C expressed on mucosal epithelial cells, resulting 
in the production and release of cGMP , inhibiting nociceptors.37,98
Serotonin Agonists. Serotonin agonists affect 5-HT4 recep -
tors to facilitate colonic motility and intestinal secretion.
Prucalopride. Prucalopride is a selective 5-HT4 receptor ago-
nist that stimulates colonic transit and improves constipation by 
causing high-amplitude propagated contractions, hence enhanc -
ing segmental contractions.134
Tegaserod. T egaserod, a partial 5-HT4 agonist, was removed 
from the US market when it was found to have increased risk for 
cardiovascular events.
m-Receptor Antagonists. m-Receptor antagonists selectively 
block m-receptors peripherally in the GI tract, outside of the CNS.
For patients who have significant problems with constipation 
related to long-term opiate use due to its effects on m-receptors, 
there are four medications currently available. Methylnaltrexone 
(Relistor, subcutaneous), alvimopan (Entereg), and naloxegol 
(Movantik) improve constipation without reversing analgesia or 
prompting opioid withdrawal. 21,126 More recently, naldemedine 
(Symproic in the US, which is the only agent to bind to mu, 
kappa, and delta, has been shown to be effective compared to  
placebo.136
Physical Interventions
Bowel Irrigation
T ransanal irrigation with a unique enema system that includes a 
pump and a rectal balloon catheter (Peristeen) was found to be 
helpful in one large multisite randomized controlled study by 
improving constipation, reducing incontinence frequency, reduc-
ing total time for bowel care, reducing GI symptoms, and improv-
ing quality of life in individuals with SCI as compared with the 
regular bowel program. 24,25,36,76,112 Subsequent studies further 
demonstrated lower costs of care,23,25 reduced or discontinued use 
of medications,33,76 and prolonged successful outcomes. Contin -
ued use of the device can successfully prevent constipation and/or 
FI and other symptoms.36,38,76
Pulse irrigation enema (PIE) of the bowel has been shown  
to be a safe and effective method for relieving fecal loading and 
impactions by delivering intermittent, rapid pulses of warm water 
into the rectum for bowel cleansing and to stimulate rectal motil-
ity. However, the device is large and bulky and requires huge 
disposable bag collection systems.76,112
Biofeedback and Pelvic Floor Physical Therapy
When neurogenic bowel deficits are incomplete and some degree 
of control and sensation is present, pelvic floor physical therapy 
and use of biofeedback might offer a means of enhancing  
the patient’s residual sensory and motor abilities. This typically  
requires several sessions of therapy.17 Patients with SCI and NBD 
showed improvement in their sensory and motor anorectal func -
tion, including lowering of first sensation threshold and more  
effective balloon expulsion. They had improved symptom scores 
and positive effect of NBD on quality of life. 96 For individuals 
with a good degree of volitional EAS activation and some degree 
of anorectal sensation, biofeedback, and pelvic floor physical 
therapy can be a tool to help restore not only social continence 
but even normal defecatory control.96,140
Abdominal Massage
Abdominal massage has been shown to promote better bowel 
movements, relieve abdominal distention, and improve colonic 
transit time in people with SCI. This was demonstrated in  
24 subjects who received 15 minutes of abdominal massage  
from the cecum, throughout the colon, and to the rectum for  
15 days. 8,76 Constipation score was significantly improved in  
multiple sclerosis patients who received abdominal massage for  
4 weeks, although there were no changes in the NBD or quality 
of life scores. Long-term improvement was not demonstrated 
when treatment was stopped after 4 weeks. 25,97

of the abdominal muscles in people with tetraplegia was shown to

compared with placebo.
